title: Cost per Use Calculator
shortDescription: Find out what each use of an item really costs.
description: Calculate the cost per use of any purchase by dividing the price by the number of times you use it. A simple way to judge whether something is worth buying.
metaDescription: Free cost per use calculator. Divide price by number of uses to see what each use really costs and decide if a purchase is worth the money.

result.label: Cost per use
result.empty: Enter the price and number of uses to see the cost per use.

sections.heading: What is cost per use?
paragraphs: Cost per use is the price of an item divided by how many times you actually use it. A coat that costs $200 and is worn 200 times has a cost per use of just $1.
It turns a big upfront price into a small, everyday number, making it easier to compare a cheap item you rarely use against a pricier one you use all the time.

sections.heading: Why it helps you decide
paragraphs: A high sticker price can feel like a waste, but if you use the item often the cost per use can be tiny. The opposite is also true: a bargain you never touch is expensive per use.
Use this number to spot true value, avoid impulse buys, and choose items that earn their keep over time.

howTo.name: Enter the price
howTo.text: Type in what you paid, or expect to pay, for the item.

howTo.name: Enter the number of uses
howTo.text: Estimate how many times you will realistically use it over its life.

howTo.name: Read the cost per use
howTo.text: The calculator divides price by uses to show the cost of each single use.

faq.q: How is cost per use calculated?
faq.a: Cost per use equals the price divided by the number of uses. For example, $150 over 50 uses is $3 per use.

faq.q: What counts as a good cost per use?
faq.a: There is no fixed rule. Compare the cost per use to similar items or to how much value each use gives you.
For a sharper number, add cleaning, repairs, or accessories to the price before dividing by uses.

faq.q: Can I use it before buying?
faq.a: Yes. Estimate the expected number of uses to predict the cost per use and decide if a purchase is worth it.

faq.q: Does a higher price always mean worse value?
faq.a: No. A pricey item used very often can have a lower cost per use than a cheap item used rarely.
